title: Energy intensity vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Energy intensity represents [primary energy](#dod:primaryenergy) consumption, using the [substitution method](#dod:substitutionmethod), per unit of gross domestic product (GDP). GDP is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Energy intensity vs. GDP per capita, 2022

Energy intensity represents primary energy consumption, using the substitution method, per unit of gross domestic product (GDP). GDP is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between energy intensity, measured in kilowatt-hours per dollar on the vertical axis, and GDP per capita, measured in international dollars at 2011 prices on a logarithmic horizontal axis. The data points are color-coded by region, including North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. A broad distribution is visible across the chart, with no single linear trend dominating the entire field. Several countries exhibit high energy intensity above 4 kWh per dollar despite varying economic outputs; Turkmenistan, North Korea, Venezuela, and Bahrain occupy the upper section of the chart. In contrast, a cluster of nations including Denmark, Norway, Switzerland, and Ireland sits in the bottom right, characterized by GDP per capita exceeding $50,000 and energy intensity below 1.5 kWh per dollar. African nations, represented by purple dots, are largely concentrated in the lower-left quadrant, with countries like Burundi, Liberia, and Chad showing GDP per capita below $2,000 and energy intensity generally under 1 kWh per dollar, though Zimbabwe and Mozambique appear higher on the intensity scale near 2 kWh. Asian and Middle Eastern countries display significant variance; Qatar and Kuwait appear on the far right with high GDP, while Iran and Russia sit in the middle range with intensity levels between 2 and 2.5 kWh. The horizontal axis spans from $1,000 to over $100,000, allowing for the visualization of both low-income and high-income economies within the same frame.
